sections.heading: First polio doses reached many children, but later doses fell behind
sections.body: In 2025, 93.0 percent of children aged 12 to 23 months had received OPV 1. Coverage was lower for later doses: 89.5 percent for OPV 2 and 83.0 percent for OPV 3. Polio 3 coverage was 83.3 percent, and IPV coverage dropped from 89.1 percent for IPV 1 to 78.9 percent for IPV 2. This shows that early doses reached more children than later-dose completion.

sections.heading: Ilocos Region kept coverage high across the polio vaccine sequence
sections.body: Ilocos Region had 100.0 percent OPV 1 coverage in 2025 and stayed close to that level for later doses: 99.6 percent for OPV 2, 97.7 percent for OPV 3, and 98.0 percent for Polio 3. IPV coverage was also high, at 98.8 percent for IPV 1 and 97.5 percent for IPV 2.

sections.heading: BARMM had the lowest coverage, especially for later polio doses
sections.body: BARMM was far below other regions in 2025. Coverage was 63.3 percent for OPV 1 and 29.7 percent for both OPV 3 and Polio 3. IPV coverage also fell sharply, from 52.2 percent for IPV 1 to 30.2 percent for IPV 2. This is an inference, but these large gaps suggest that BARMM faced major challenges in both reaching children for the first dose and completing later doses.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ trailed most regions and was lower than in 2022 on some measures
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula recorded 77.6 percent OPV 1 coverage in 2025. Coverage then declined to 73.9 percent for OPV 2 and 66.3 percent for OPV 3 and Polio 3. Its 2025 levels were also lower than its 2022 levels for OPV 3, which was 77.5 percent in 2022, and for Polio 3, which was 71.9 percent in 2022.

sections.heading: In some regions, OPV coverage stayed higher than IPV 2 coverage
sections.body: Western Visayas reached 98.6 percent for OPV 1 and 93.5 percent for Polio 3 in 2025, but its IPV 2 coverage was lower at 69.6 percent. Northern Mindanao showed a similar split, with 85.2 percent for OPV 1 and 76.3 percent for Polio 3, compared with 54.2 percent for IPV 2. This pattern suggests that completing OPV and completing IPV did not always move together across regions.

sections.heading: Girls had slightly higher coverage than boys, but the gap was small
sections.body: Female children had slightly higher 2025 coverage than male children across the listed polio measures. Polio 3 coverage was 84.3 percent among female children and 82.5 percent among male children. OPV 1 coverage was 93.9 percent among female children and 92.4 percent among male children. This sex gap was smaller than the national gap between OPV 1 and Polio 3.

sections.heading: Children with higher birth order had lower polio completion
sections.body: In 2025, Polio 3 coverage was about the same for first-born children and children of birth order 2 to 3, then fell for children with higher birth order. Coverage was 84.7 percent among first-born children and 85.3 percent among children of birth order 2 to 3, then fell to 78.3 percent for birth order 4 to 5 and 70.4 percent for birth order 6 or higher. OPV 1 coverage also declined across the same range, from 93.7 percent for first-born children to 88.5 percent for birth order 6 or higher.

sections.heading: Children with a seen vaccination card had much higher coverage
sections.body: Children whose vaccination card was seen had very high 2025 coverage: 98.9 percent for OPV 1, 96.1 percent for OPV 3, and 96.5 percent for Polio 3. Coverage was much lower among children who never had a card, at 45.2 percent for OPV 1 and 25.5 percent for both OPV 3 and Polio 3. This is an inference, but card status appears to distinguish children with documented contact from children who were much less likely to complete the sequence.

sections.heading: Urban coverage was slightly higher than rural coverage
sections.body: In 2025, urban children had higher coverage than rural children on every listed polio measure. For OPV 1, the gap was 1.9 percentage points: 94.0 percent in urban areas and 92.1 percent in rural areas. For Polio 3, the gap was 3.5 percentage points: 85.0 percent in urban areas and 81.5 percent in rural areas.

sections.heading: Coverage was lower for children whose mothers had Grades 1-6 education
sections.body: Among children whose mothers had Grades 1-6 education, 2025 coverage was 85.5 percent for OPV 1 and 71.7 percent for both OPV 3 and Polio 3. Children whose mothers had college education had higher coverage: 93.7 percent for OPV 1, 85.4 percent for OPV 3, and 85.8 percent for Polio 3. Rows for mothers with no education were suppressed in 2025 and are not treated as zero.

sections.heading: Wealth gaps were larger for later-dose completion
sections.body: In 2025, Polio 3 coverage was 75.2 percent among children in the lowest wealth quintile, compared with 90.9 percent in the highest quintile. OPV 1 coverage also differed, at 88.0 percent in the lowest quintile and 95.3 percent in the highest quintile. The gap was larger for Polio 3 than for OPV 1, showing that later-dose completion was more unequal than early-dose receipt.

takeaways: Nationally, OPV 1 coverage was 93.0 percent in 2025, while Polio 3 coverage was lower at 83.3 percent.
Regional Polio 3 completion ranged from 98.0 percent in Ilocos Region to 29.7 percent in BARMM.
Children whose vaccination card was seen had 96.5 percent Polio 3 coverage, compared with 25.5 percent among children who never had a card.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
